quick_facts.label: Degree type
quick_facts.value: A Master of Finance (MFin)—also offered under related names such as Master in International Finance—is a specialised postgraduate degree centred on finance. Exact programme design, entry profile and award title differ by school.

quick_facts.label: GMAT relevance
quick_facts.value: GMAT policies are school-specific: MIT Sloan lists GMAT/GRE submission as optional for its 2026 MFin application, whereas HEC Paris requires an approved management test (GMAT, GRE or TAGE MAGE) for its Master in International Finance. Applicants should verify the policy for the intended intake and programme rather than assume that GMAT is universally required.

quick_facts.label: Typical study length
quick_facts.value: Full-time formats can be approximately one year, but vary: MIT Sloan offers 12- and 18-month MFin routes, while Cambridge Judge lists its MFin as a one-year, full-time programme.

quick_facts.label: Work-experience profile
quick_facts.value: Some programmes target recent graduates, while others are post-experience. HEC Paris accepts applicants holding a bachelor’s or master’s degree with a strong quantitative background; Cambridge’s 2026-entry MFin guidance calls for at least two years of post-graduation finance or finance-related work experience by programme start.

sections.heading: Eligibility and academic preparation
paragraphs: There is no single overseas-MFin eligibility rule. A common baseline is a completed undergraduate degree, but schools may evaluate the academic record, quantitative preparation, English proficiency and professional experience differently. For example, HEC Paris asks applicants to hold a bachelor’s or master’s degree from an international institution, be fluent in English and have a strong quantitative background; its Business Track additionally expects introductory knowledge of accounting, corporate finance, investments, derivatives, business statistics and calculus.

Cambridge Judge illustrates the post-experience variant: its MFin application guidance states that applicants should have a strong bachelor’s record and at least two years of graduate work experience in a core finance or finance-related role by the programme start. Thus, an Indian applicant should distinguish between pre-experience MFin/MSc Finance courses and programmes designed for working finance professionals before investing in GMAT preparation or applications.
bullets: Check whether the programme accepts a three-year Indian bachelor’s degree and how it assesses its equivalence; the school’s country-specific admissions guidance is controlling.
Build evidence of quantitative readiness through transcripts and relevant coursework in mathematics, statistics, economics, accounting, programming or finance where required.
Plan for English-language evidence when it is not waived; HEC lists TOEFL, TOEIC, IELTS or Cambridge tests, while Cambridge lists accepted English-proficiency routes separately.
Treat work experience as a programme-specific condition, not a universal MFin requirement.

sections.heading: Duration and mode of study
paragraphs: MFin study abroad is commonly full-time, but the calendar and opportunities for internships differ. MIT Sloan states that its degree requirements can be completed in 18 months or accelerated to 12 months; the 18-month route may allow an internship or research assistantship in the second summer. Cambridge Judge describes its MFin as full-time for one year, beginning in September 2026.

Applicants should compare the published academic calendar, internship window, visa implications and whether a programme permits part-time work. HEC Paris states that attendance is mandatory throughout its Master in International Finance and that the programme is not compatible with part-time work.
bullets: 12 months: a compressed format may suit applicants seeking a faster return to work.
18 months or longer: may create additional space for a summer internship, research, electives or career transition activities.
Confirm the specific intake start date and completion date directly with the university before arranging funding or visa documentation.

sections.heading: What students study
paragraphs: The curriculum is typically quantitative and applied rather than a general-management MBA curriculum. MIT Sloan describes required core courses, restricted electives and an action-learning course. Its stated foundations include modern finance, financial mathematics and financial accounting, alongside electives that can cover areas such as fintech, quantitative methods, fixed income, mergers and acquisitions, asset management and healthcare finance.

Specialisation pathways vary. HEC Paris structures its Master in International Finance around Accelerated or Business tracks, then Capital Markets or Corporate Finance specialisations, followed by advanced core courses and electives. Its published structure also includes a research paper and four to six months of professional experience through an internship and/or full-time employment.
bullets: Core concepts commonly include corporate finance, capital markets, valuation, accounting, investments, risk and financial economics.
Quantitative preparation can include probability, statistics, optimisation, programming and financial mathematics.
Applied components may include a finance lab, industry project, research paper, career workshops or internship; these are not identical across schools.
Compare required modules—not only programme titles—if choosing between MFin, MSc Finance, financial engineering and international-finance degrees.

sections.heading: GMAT and the admissions process
paragraphs: A GMAT score can be relevant for this node, but it is not a universal admission requirement. MIT Sloan says GMAT (Focus Edition or 10th Edition) or GRE submission is optional for its 2026 MFin application, while encouraging candidates with a score to submit it as an additional data point. By contrast, HEC Paris lists an official GMAT, GRE or TAGE MAGE score report among its Master in International Finance application documents and says no minimum score is required to apply.

A typical application also includes academic transcripts, degree documentation, CV/resumé, English-language evidence where applicable, references, essays or statements, and sometimes an interview. HEC Paris describes a two-stage process comprising application review followed by a face-to-face or Zoom interview for pre-selected candidates. Cambridge asks for transcripts, evidence of professional development/work experience, English evidence where required, and two references.

Deadlines and test policies change by intake. For context, MIT states that its 2026-entry deadline has passed, and Cambridge says applications for September 2027 entry will open on 1 September 2026. These dates should not be extrapolated to other universities or later cycles.
bullets: First shortlist programmes by career stage, quantitative prerequisites, location, cost and work-authorisation considerations.
Read each school’s current test policy before booking the GMAT; a strong GMAT may be useful even where optional, but it does not replace required academic or language evidence.
Request transcripts and references early, and allow time for test-score reporting and visa documentation.
Use only the intake year published by the university when planning application rounds and deposits.

sections.heading: Fees, living costs and funding
paragraphs: Costs are highly school-, country- and intake-specific, and tuition is only one part of the budget. As published for the 2026–27 academic year, MIT Sloan lists MFin tuition of US$96,884 for its 12-month format and US$133,007 for its 18-month format. Cambridge Judge lists £60,000 tuition for its 2026/27 MFin. HEC Paris lists total tuition fees of €47,500 for 2026/27 for its Master in International Finance, with a stated additional €2,000 charge applicable to international students; its page labels the fees as indicative and subject to change.

Students should separately budget for accommodation, food, health insurance, travel, visa-related charges, books or equipment, local transport and currency movement. HEC Paris estimates remaining living costs at approximately €1,500 per month, but this is programme-specific context rather than a universal European budget. Scholarships, bursaries, institutional aid and external funding may be available, but eligibility, amounts and timing vary.
bullets: Use the university’s official cost-of-attendance or fees page for the intended year; do not rely on rankings pages, coaching estimates or old tuition figures.
Identify whether fees cover student services, insurance, course materials, residence, travel or internship-related costs.
Review deposit deadlines and refund terms before accepting an offer; HEC Paris states that admitted students confirm their place with a non-refundable deposit that is deducted from total tuition.
Plan the budget in the fee currency and retain contingency for exchange-rate changes.

sections.heading: Possible next steps after the degree
paragraphs: Graduates may use an MFin to pursue finance roles or further academic study, but outcomes depend on prior experience, visa/work-authorisation rules, local hiring conditions and individual performance. HEC Paris frames its programme around capital-markets and corporate-finance career paths, and its curriculum requires a research paper plus professional experience. MIT’s curriculum includes action learning with corporate partners and allows a thesis or independent study with faculty approval.

For applicants considering doctoral study, confirm whether the intended degree carries sufficient research training and credential recognition. HEC Paris explains that its 120-ECTS programme can bring the cumulative total to 300 ECTS for students with 180 ECTS from a bachelor’s degree, enabling progression to doctoral study in that system; this is a programme- and jurisdiction-specific example, not a guarantee of PhD admission.
bullets: Career route: use electives, projects, internships and the career office to target a finance specialism aligned with prior preparation.
Academic route: prioritise programmes offering research methods, a supervised research paper, thesis options and faculty fit if a PhD is a serious goal.
Professional route: compare whether the degree complements—not substitutes for—relevant work experience and any professional credentials sought in the target market.
